scenario: Quanta Healthcare runs a Camunda 8.8 patient triage workflow that processes 4000 instances per day. An ad-hoc sub-process is implemented with a job worker that decides which inner steps (lab order, imaging, consult) to activate based on each patient profile and continues across multiple turns.
question: How does the job worker control which inner elements run, across multiple activations of the ad-hoc sub-process?

options.text: The worker writes the next element ID into a process variable, and Zeebe polls that variable on every tick to activate the matching inner element
The worker calls a dedicated REST endpoint per inner element, and Zeebe activates the element that was last requested by the worker until completion
The worker completes the job with an adHocSubProcess result that lists element IDs, and Zeebe creates a new job for the sub-process when a flow inside finishes
The worker subscribes to a Kafka topic of ad-hoc events, and Zeebe activates the inner element whose ID matches the latest event arriving on that topic

a.text: Incorrect. Zeebe does not poll process variables to trigger ad-hoc activation; the job worker interacts through job creation and completion, not by writing into a shared variable.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no dedicated REST endpoint pattern per inner element. The worker communicates with Zeebe through one job for the ad-hoc sub-process at a time.

c.text: Correct. The worker completes the job with an adHocSubProcess job result that names the element IDs to activate, and Zeebe creates a new job when any inner flow completes.

d.text: Incorrect. Kafka topics are not part of the ad-hoc sub-process job protocol. Activation happens through completing the job with an adHocSubProcess job result.

explanation: When an ad-hoc sub-process is defined as a job worker, Zeebe creates a job, the worker decides which elements to activate by completing it with an adHocSubProcess result, and Zeebe recreates the job as inner flows complete.
